## [15:15 - 18:17]

look at my CPMs for this right now, my scaling, so my purchase optimized events are giving me a $25 CPM. So that's kind of okay for Q4. But because my click-through rate is kind of good, I'm getting fairly decent cost per click. So 2.34% for cold traffic optimized for purchase is actually really, really good. And that bottoms out with a good cost per outbound click or okay. It can definitely be better. But that means that actually when I'm running this, if I can get my cost per out to cart down, bring my cost per initial check out down cost my purchase down with our current AOV, which I think is about $35, $40. Once I've got the sales funnel working, I now have a good idea of my interests and my ads that are working. Then I work on AOV and I know I can get that AOV up from 40 to 50, 60. At that point, once my traffic is running smoothly, once my sales funnel is running smoothly, then I'm scaling that up and I'm working on backend initiatives to get my AOV up, maybe cart-based AOV objectives as well. But that's the kind of key thing that I want you to be thinking about. So when you're running your E-com, first of all, figure out who your best interests are or your kind of best audiences, if you've got look alike, if you've got custom data already, then great. Then figure out your best ads. And that's through graduation testing. Once you've got that and you don't have to spend a ton, you can do that in a more controlled manner. It depends how fast you want those results. Then test those with the purchase event and see what kind of results you can get through, how far people are going through your funnel, what kind of numbers you're getting in your cost per view content, cost-practice cart, and then through your funnel. And figure out if you have a problem, some sites that I work on, they just flow. I don't have a problem converting and I'm getting good ROAS straight after graduating. In some cases like this, I know because it's print on demand and there's some customization, I need to work harder on the proposition on the product page. And now I'm reducing my budget down, I'm reducing my test budget down, and I'm going to focus more heavily on how I can actually squeeze better conversion rates through A-B testing, through surveys, through analysis, question asking, et cetera, and actually get more value out before I start to ramp up that spend and see it bottom out with better performance. So in terms of structure, that's the kind of focus. I would also say have your retargeting running as well. I've actually got two campaigns running, one I've literally just launched so it doesn't have any data. What I've actually done, and this is actually a good point for your retargeting, is when it comes to kind of low-ticket items, so this is $35, $40, $50, which is for me free, it's still quite low-ticket. There's two sides of retargeting. Do you want to retarget someone who's just visited your site and hit them straight up

## [18:17 - 21:17]

with a retargeting ad? In some cases, fine, you want to be super aggressive, you want to go for it, that's fine. The problem is when you're running cold traffic and someone clicks into your site, they then disappear, they see a retargeting ad and then they buy anyway. It looks like your retargeting is actually bringing in the sour, but in reality they're going to buy anyway. So the way I negate this is actually I don't retarget someone on the first day, especially early on while I'm still gathering data. I wait until the second day after they click, then I retarget them. So if they haven't bought on the first day naturally through the cold ad, then on the second day I start my retargeting. And actually when it comes to retargeting, I don't go straight for all, split the first few days and do offers and the next seven days do offers, etc., etc. Actually leave that quite broad. And then the ads within that, so this ad set here is retargeting to people between two and 30 days after the first click into the View Content or the Atacart event. I'm keeping it broad because I want bigger audiences. This is another mistake that a lot of people make, especially when you don't have enough traffic is to super segment a small audience. Your CPMs go up, your results fluctuate, it's hard to get consistency. So I would keep that audience broad. So I've gone for View Content and Atacart. Anyone that's hit any of those stages will get this ad and keep it broad in terms of two to 30 days, all placements, broad gender, age, all that kind of stuff. And then what I'm doing within there is testing multiple ads and offers to see what actually works. So you can see at the moment I'm getting a $7 Cospr Intercom, $15 Cospr Intercom check out. So that's a roughly 50% conversion rate from Atacart to check out. And then $15 to $20 there means my conversion rate is a lot higher there. My conversion rate is bottoming out at 1.68 romance. That for me is not good enough. That is kind of what I want from my code. I want my retargeting to be double that because that means that my retargeting is working a lot stronger. Now, if I go back and I kind of look at the kind of other metrics that are coming through and I look at my click-through rate, for example, so my click-through rate for retargeting is really still quite strong, 4.39%. So I know that that's good. So my ads are doing a good job. But somewhere along the line, we're still failing to convert enough people to go ahead and buy the product. And again, the same problem here, as I explained previously, is the number of people viewing the product page and actually getting into the car is the problem. So I know that with my cold audience and my retargeting, if I can fix up my product page, honestly, I think I can double raw ass here as well as in my cold. And this is the thing about Facebook ads is when people say, you know, Facebook ads is a problem, often it's not. It's your funnel. And the challenge is figuring out where in the funnel the problem is and looking at numbers in the right way. And you can see the way I've set my columns.

## [21:17 - 24:18]

I'm looking at the actual data that's coming through. Then I'm looking at the cost for getting people to each other's stages and making decision space on that. I've launched an offer here, which is anyone that's clicked between five and 10 days and not necessarily purchased. I'm trying to get them back into the site. And this is a dynamic product ad. And what I want to do is actually push an offer. So I've got a 10% offer here for anyone that hasn't purchased within five days of their first click or within 10 days of the first click, I'm pushing a 10% offer. Why wait five days? Why not do it two, three days? This is just testing. So I'm going to test five to 10 days, see what kind of data I get back. And then I might adjust that window and maybe do three to five days or three to 10 days, three to 15, whatever it is. And I want to see what data comes back. So if you've read, like for example, you should always run 10%, three to five days and 15% five to 10 days. I'm telling you right now, that might have worked for someone else, but it might probably want to work for you. You need to test what's going to work for you. And that's what I'm doing here. So I'm testing five to 10 days, see what kind of data I get back. And if that doesn't work, I'll adjust it. The reason I've got a dynamic product catalog here and I've got all products over $20 is when it comes to retargeting, if you're selling accessories, no matter what store you're running, if you're selling accessories and you've got $5 products, $2, $10, $12, etc. And you don't want to drag your AOV down and you don't want to waste valuable dynamic product at carousel space with cheaper items, then create a dynamic catalog, which you'll see in the DPA part of the training, which has products which are over a certain value. So for me, I don't want to surface any products that are less than $20. And I have products in this particular catalog below that. So I've just set 20 as a threshold. The average selling price on the site is anywhere between $25 and $40. I want those products to be surfaced in my retargeting. So for you, it's making sure if you do want to run dynamic product ads and retarget people that visit the site, do you want to surface your whole catalog? If you're running ranges and your prices across your products are pretty much all the same and that's what you want, then that's perfect. Then push your whole catalog back in retargeting. But if, like me, you don't want those cheap products surfaced in retargeting ads, then set your minimum price and create a sub-catalog, which again, you'll see in the DPA part of the training. So that's the kind of campaign structure in terms of the kinds of ads for retargeting. I'll be covering those off in different part of the training, but this is just really talking through and this is a slightly longer video to go through a campaign structure and how I would go from testing to scaling. I've set up retargeting. You should always do that as early as possible. And some of the decisions that have gone into trying to pull out the best performing campaigns

## [24:18 - 26:58]

and to start building out as the data comes in. And here's another example here. So as I started to get more video views coming in, I've tested different audiences, but for Worm. So Worm is, in this instance, it's kind of fans and people that engage with the video. When I talk about the full funnel system, it's not about setting every part of it up and getting it all running at the same time. So right now, we don't have an existing customer campaign running. The next step for me would be now that sales are coming in within a certain period, start running ads to those customers and see if I can actually bump up the lifetime value as fast as possible. You can do that obviously through your e-commerce application and run post-purchase sequence, run that absolutely through email as well and try and bump that value up. Because again, not that I'm aiming for these numbers, but for example, if I was getting a 0.75 or 75% rise on my cold traffic, if my goal was to just break even at the front but I wanted to profit off the back end, then I know that I could just squeeze this a bit more and then I can really start scanning that traffic up because I've got emails running, maybe messenger campaigns and also post-purchase Facebook ads campaigns, which you can see in that part of the training for post-purchase. Then I can start ramping up and I know that my back end is already in place and I start tweaking there. So when it comes to e-commerce, there's just so many components that you actually have to test in a structured way and get right. And this is my approach. So really, I start at the front, I'm testing for ads, I'm testing for audiences, and then I'm seeing if I can actually generate sales. Then I'm looking at where the potential pinch points are that I need to do conversion rate optimization. Then I'm looking to see if I can bump up my average order value. Then I'm looking at my back end to see if I can bump up my lifetime value. So if you take away anything from this training and hopefully, if you're listening to this, you've been through the 25 minutes or so and kind of seen the back part of this, absolutely focus on getting parts of it done step by step. If you have a team to do it, you can do it all at the same time. If you're doing this yourself, do it in a bit more of a structured fashion and then you'll start to see better performance come through and you can make the right decision at the right time. And if you're in the private mastermind, then post your questions in. Let me help you to kind of make sense of your data because especially if you're newer to e-com and you haven't really run Facebook ads successfully in the past, making sure that you understand the data is critical so you know where to make those decision points. Make sure you set those columns up in a similar way to I have and you make it so much easier to make those decisions and be able to start to make more profit earlier on.
